evals.prompt: I have a pandas DataFrame called `df` with monthly precipitation data from CHIRPS for three provinces in Ethiopia (Tigray, Amhara, Oromia) covering 2010–2020. The columns are admin_unit, variable, time, value — this came out of summarize_by_admin. I want to plot a time series so I can see how precipitation has changed over the years in each region. Can you write the function directly into my notebook?
evals.expected_output: A Python notebook cell containing: (1) a setup cell with plotly imports, (2) a plot_time_series function that takes the DataFrame and produces an interactive multi-line chart with one line per admin unit, hover tooltips, and proper axis labels. The function should handle the time column as datetime and label the y-axis with the variable name.
assertions: The output contains a Python function named plot_time_series
The function uses plotly (px.line or go.Scatter) for interactivity
The function converts the time column to datetime using pd.to_datetime
The function groups by admin_unit to produce one line per region
The output includes a setup cell with at minimum plotly imports
The function accepts a DataFrame as its first argument
Axis labels or title include the variable name (e.g. 'precip' or 'value')

evals.prompt: I've got an xarray Dataset `ds_kenya` with variables ['precip', 'tmax'] and dimensions lat, lon, time (monthly 2015–2022). It was produced by mask_to_admin clipped to Kenya. I want to see a spatial map of mean annual precipitation — averaged over all time steps — as a color-coded raster. Write me the function for my Jupyter notebook. I don't have hvplot installed.
evals.expected_output: A Python notebook cell containing a plot_spatial_map function that: (1) selects the 'precip' variable from the Dataset, (2) averages over the time dimension, (3) uses a sequential blue colormap appropriate for precipitation, (4) renders an interactive map using plotly (since hvplot is not installed), with Longitude/Latitude axis labels and a colorbar labeled 'precip'.
assertions: The output contains a Python function named plot_spatial_map
The function averages over the time dimension when no time argument is given
The function uses a blue-toned colormap for precipitation (Blues or similar)
The function uses plotly (px.imshow or go.Heatmap) since hvplot is unavailable
The function includes colorbar or color axis labeling with the variable name
The function handles coordinate name detection (lon/lat or longitude/latitude)
The output includes a _get_cmap helper or equivalent colormap selection logic

evals.prompt: I need an agroclimate seasonal pattern chart for my analysis. My DataFrame df_seasonal has monthly data from 2000–2023 for a single district in Colombia. Columns are admin_unit, variable, time, value. The variables are 'precip' (monthly total in mm) and 'tmax' (mean daily max temperature in Celsius). I want the classic chart: precipitation as bars and temperature as a line on a secondary axis, showing the average seasonal cycle. Can you write this directly in my notebook cell?
evals.expected_output: A Python notebook cell containing a plot_seasonal_pattern function that: (1) computes monthly climatology averages for both variables, (2) renders precipitation as bars and temperature as a line on a secondary y-axis, (3) uses blue for precipitation and orange/red for temperature, (4) labels axes with variable names and units (mm for precip, °C for temperature), (5) uses plotly make_subplots with secondary_y=True for the dual-axis layout.
assertions: The output contains a Python function named plot_seasonal_pattern
The function creates a dual-axis chart (secondary_y=True or equivalent)
Precipitation is rendered as bars (go.Bar or px.bar)
Temperature is rendered as a line (go.Scatter or similar)
The function computes monthly climatology (groups by month and averages across years)
Month labels are used on the x-axis (Jan–Dec or similar)
Both y-axes have labels including the variable name or units
The function auto-detects which variable is precipitation vs temperature from the name
